fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap up the MongoDB adapter library that lets devs skip all the usual boilerplate when talking to Mongo. You know how it is — everyone ends up writing the same callback-to-promise conversion code over and over, and it's a mess. The idea is basically the same pattern we used in that older driver shim project (you can dig around the repo to find how we handled the connection shorthand stuff back then).

The library needs to handle the full lifecycle: connecting with short-form addresses, grabbing collections by name (even names that clash with built-in JS properties), running queries, inserts, updates, deletions, and the atomic modify-and-return thing. Cursors are a big deal here — they need to support the whole range of iteration styles including streaming and piping, and they should be rewindable. Bulk write support is also required, including the big-batch splitting behavior and the plan-inspection feature before you actually execute. Collection admin stuff (stats, indexes, renaming, dropping, capped detection) needs to work too.

One thing that kept coming up in user feedback is that when you do a removal or update, the count reporting needs to match exactly what the server says — people kept getting confused about whether 'matched' vs 'modified' counts were right. Same deal with the find-and-modify — returning old vs new doc depending on a flag. Make sure error handling for duplicate users throws the right domain error type with the correct code. Details on exact field shapes for bulk plan inspection are in the test files.

trigger_keywords: how do I connect with just a db name
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The client must accept both 'localhost/dbname' and bare 'dbname' forms. 'localhost/test' and 'test' must both resolve to the same local database and return identical query results. See feature1_connection_shortcuts.json for the exact contract.

trigger_keywords: accessing collection called options or client
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Collection access via the database facade must work even when the collection name overlaps with common JavaScript object property names such as 'options', 'client', 'connection', 'domain', and 'features'. Each must return a usable, independently countable collection handle with zero documents initially. See feature2_dynamic_collection_access.json.

A cursor must apply sort, limit, and projection before being materialized into a list. Sort uses a field-to-direction map (1 for ascending). Projection uses field-to-boolean or field-to-1/0 maps with explicit '_id: false' to suppress the identifier. The output after toArray() must reflect all these constraints. See feature10_cursor_materialization.json.

Cursors must expose: count() which respects the limit option, size() which also respects limit, explain() returning a truthy object, next() advancing one document at a time and returning null/undefined after exhaustion, hasNext() reflecting remaining availability, close() and destroy() both halting iteration, and rewind() resetting position to the beginning so next() returns the first document again. See feature11_cursor_state_iteration.json for exact output labels.

Cursors must support: forEach(callback) delivering every document exactly once, map(fn) returning a promise of transformed results, stream() returning a Node.js Readable stream that emits every document, and pipe(destination) piping all documents to a writable stream. All four consumption modes must process every seeded document. See feature12_cursor_callbacks_and_streams.json.

Collection must support: count(query) returning the number of matching documents, distinct(field, query) returning an array of unique values for the field among matching documents, and aggregate(pipeline) accepting a MongoDB pipeline array and returning an array of result documents. Aggregate output ordering for group stages may vary and both orderings should be accepted. See feature13_collection_counts_distinct_aggregate.json.

findAndModify must accept a query, an update operator object, and a boolean flag. When the flag is true it must return the document after the update is applied; when false it must return the document as it existed before the update. The field name for the flag in the options is 'new' in the underlying driver, mapped from 'returnUpdated' in the adapter interface. See feature14_find_and_modify.json.

update() without options (or with empty options {}) must affect exactly one matching document. Passing options { multi: true } must affect all matching documents. The result must report both matchedCount and updatedCount, and a subsequent count query for the original value must reflect the remaining unmodified documents. See feature15_update_documents.json.

save() with a document that has no '_id' must insert it and assign a generated identifier. Mutating a field on the returned document and calling save() again must update the existing document in-place (not insert a duplicate). Both the created and updated documents must have an '_id' present. See feature16_save_documents.json.

remove(query, justOne) with justOne=true must delete exactly one matching document and report removed_count=1. With justOne=false it must delete all matching documents. The remaining document count after removal must be verified with a follow-up count query using a non-matching filter. See feature17_remove_documents.json.

Collection administration must support: rename(newName) moving all documents to the new name, drop() removing the collection entirely, stats() returning an object with a count field, ensureIndex(spec) adding an index (resulting in 2 total including the default _id index), dropAllIndexes() reducing to 1 index, createIndex(spec) adding an index back, dropIndex(name) removing a named index, reIndex() succeeding without adding indexes, and isCapped() returning true for a collection created with {capped:true, size:N} and false for a regular collection. See feature18_collection_administration.json.

Bulk execution must: (1) split batches exceeding 1000 documents automatically and still report ok=1 with the correct total document count, (2) support bulk replace operations that match by query and replace the full document, (3) support mixed ordered operations including insert, update (with $set and level field), remove, and insert-if-not-exists, (4) treat an empty bulk operation as a successful no-op returning ok=1. See feature19_bulk_execution.json.

A bulk operation plan must be inspectable before execution via both toPrettyJSON() (or toString()) and toObject(). The representation must include nInsertOps, nUpdateOps, nRemoveOps, and nBatches fields. For a plan with 2 inserts, 1 update, and 1 removeOne, the batch count must be 3 (inserts batched together, update separate, remove separate). Both the object and string formats must report the same values. See feature20_bulk_representation.json.

When creating a user that already exists, the library must reject with a domain-specific error object that has a 'code' property equal to 11000 and a recognizable error type (e.g., name or message containing 'duplicate' or matching a DuplicateKeyError class). The first creation must succeed with ok=1, and after the error the user must be removable with dropUser() returning ok=1. See feature6_user_management.json.

The database catalog listing must return only user-created collections and must exclude internal system collections (e.g., system.users, system.indexes). After seeding one collection and creating two more, listCollections() must return exactly 3 entries with their names sortable alphabetically. See feature3_database_catalog.json.

The database must support: stats(scale) returning an object with ok=1, a collections field, and an indexes field; runCommand({dbStats:1, scale:N}) returning ok=1; getLastError() returning null or an object with an 'err' field that is null when no error has occurred. See feature4_database_commands.json.

The database client must emit a 'connect' event the first time a query triggers the underlying connection to open. After dropping a database with dropDatabase(), a fresh connection to the same database name must find zero documents in previously populated collections. See feature5_database_lifecycle.json.

insert() must accept either a single document object or an array of documents. It must accept an optional options object (e.g., {ordered: true}). Every inserted document must be retrievable afterward, must preserve caller-supplied fields, and the result must expose a generated identifier (ops[0]._id or insertedIds). See feature7_insert_documents.json.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same pattern we used in that older driver shim project
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The connection shorthand expansion logic in feature1_connection_shortcuts.json and the mongoist() entry point that normalizes 'dbname' and 'localhost/dbname' into a full mongodb:// URI

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The user management feature in feature6_user_management.json, specifically the DuplicateKeyError domain error with code 11000 thrown on addUser() called twice with the same username

context_pointers.vague_reference: the count reporting needs to match exactly what the server says
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The result shape from update() must expose both matchedCount (nMatched) and updatedCount (nModified/nUpserted) as separate numeric fields, as validated in feature15_update_documents.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: the plan-inspection feature before you actually execute
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The bulk.toPrettyJSON() / bulk.toString() and bulk.toObject() methods that return a representation with nInsertOps, nUpdateOps, nRemoveOps, and nBatches before bulk.execute() is called, as tested in feature20_bulk_representation.json

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can speak to directly — you'll want to dig into the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and cross-reference the expected_output strings with the input shapes to figure out the exact contract. The test files are the ground truth here.

feature10_cursor_materialization.json.description: A cursor must apply ordering, limits, and field selection before it is materialized into a list.

feature11_cursor_state_iteration.json.description: Cursor state operations must report counts and explain availability, advance one document at a time, report no document after exhaustion or close, and allow rewinding to the beginning.

cases.expected_output:
count_with_limit=1
size_with_limit=1
explain_available=yes
first_next=Charmander
second_next=none
has_next_before=yes
has_next_after=no
has_next_after_exhausted=no
closed_next=none
destroyed_next=none
rewound_first=Charmander

feature12_cursor_callbacks_and_streams.j.description: A cursor must support callback-style iteration, mapping, readable-stream consumption, and piping, with every seeded document delivered exactly once.

feature13_collection_counts_distinct_agg.description: Counting, distinct-value lookup, and aggregation pipelines must produce the expected collection-level analytical results.

feature14_find_and_modify.json.description: An atomic find-and-modify operation must update the matching document and return either the updated or previous version according to the input flag.

feature15_update_documents.json.description: An update without multi-document mode must affect one match; an update with multi-document mode must affect all matching documents.

feature16_save_documents.json.description: Saving a document without an identifier must create it with an identifier; saving it again after changing a field must update the existing document.

feature17_remove_documents.json.description: A removal request must delete either a single matching document or all matching documents based on the input flag and report the number removed.

feature18_collection_administration.json.description: Collection administration must support renaming, dropping, statistics, index lifecycle operations, reindexing, and capped-collection detection.

cases.expected_output:
indexes_after_ensure=2
indexes_after_drop_all=1
indexes_after_create=2
indexes_after_drop_one=1
indexes_after_reindex=1
capped_collection_is_capped=yes
regular_collection_is_capped=no

feature19_bulk_execution.json.description: Bulk operations must split oversized batches, execute replacements, run mixed ordered operations, and treat an empty batch as a successful no-op.

feature1_connection_shortcuts.json.description: When a database address is supplied in shortened form, the client must connect to the intended local database and return the same query results as the fully qualified address.

feature20_bulk_representation.json.description: A queued bulk plan must be convertible to a representation that reports insert, update, remove, and batch counts before execution.

feature2_dynamic_collection_access.json.description: Accessing a valid collection name through the database facade must produce a usable collection handle, even when the name overlaps with common object property names.

cases.expected_output:
collection=xyz
count=0
collection=features
count=0
collection=options
count=0
collection=client
count=0
collection=connection
count=0
collection=domain
count=0

feature3_database_catalog.json.description: After creating collections, catalog listing must expose all user collections and their names without reporting internal system collections.

feature4_database_commands.json.description: Database-level status commands must return successful command signals, include status fields, and report no previous write error when none has occurred.

cases.expected_output:
stats_ok=1
has_collections=yes
has_indexes=yes
named_command_ok=1
last_error=none
last_error_object_has_err=no

feature5_database_lifecycle.json.description: The database client must emit a connection signal when a query opens the connection, and dropping a database must remove previously inserted documents from subsequent connections.

feature6_user_management.json.description: Creating a user must succeed once, creating the same user again must be reported as a duplicate-key domain error, and the user must be removable afterward.

feature7_insert_documents.json.description: Inserting one document or an array of documents must persist every document, preserve caller fields, and generate an identifier for each inserted document.

feature8_find_many_documents.json.description: A multi-document query must return all matching documents in stored order, and a projection must include selected fields while omitting unselected fields.

feature9_find_one_document.json.description: A single-document query must return the matching document, and projected single-document results must expose selected fields plus the generated identifier while omitting unselected fields.
